abstract: Smart tourism increasingly relies on health data to personalise services, yet tourists face a privacy paradox shaped by both opportunity and risk. This study investigates how system design and individual factors jointly shape health data disclosure in smart tourism. Grounded in Privacy Calculus Theory and the Antecedents–Privacy Concerns–Outcomes model, we conducted a 2 × 2 experiment (N = 412) manipulating algorithmic transparency and eConsent usability, while measuring privacy fatigue, perceived data control, trust in artificial intelligence, and digital health literacy. Using partial least squares structural equation modelling, results reveal that transparent algorithms and user-friendly consent interfaces significantly enhance both perceived control and trust, which in turn increase disclosure intention. Privacy fatigue substantially undermines these effects, yet digital health literacy buffers its negative impact on perceived control. Notably, trust in artificial intelligence emerged as the strongest driver of disclosure intention, exceeding cognitive assessments of control. These findings extend privacy calculus research by integrating emotional and cognitive mechanisms and by demonstrating the moderating role of literacy in digital health contexts. For practice, the study underscores the need for transparent design, intuitive consent, and user education to foster responsible data sharing in smart tourism ecosystems.

ur.ai_reason: 实验涉及算法透明度与AI信任，属HCI用户评估范畴
ur.tldr: 智能旅游场景下健康数据披露受系统设计与个体因素共同影响。通过2×2实验(N=412)发现：算法透明化与易用性同意界面显著提升用户感知控制与AI信任，二者进而增加数据披露意愿；隐私疲劳削弱上述效应，但数字健康素养能缓冲其对感知控制的负面影响；AI信任是披露意愿的最强驱动因素，超越认知层面的控制评估。

title: Governing health data disclosure in smart tourism: the roles of transparency, consent usability, and privacy fatigue
title_zh: 智慧旅游中健康数据披露的治理：透明度、知情同意可用性与隐私疲劳的作用
